Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside time each day, but with limited resources at home and my extracurriculars, things rarely went as planned. I'd usually grab a few hours in the morning before my anxiety kicked in too hard, then try to watch lecture videos later. Some weeks I managed to put in close to 28 hours, but it felt scattered rather than organized. I relied heavily on visual materials like diagrams and charts to make sense of things quickly.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. Early in the semester I tried to be consistent, but as stress built up, I'd end up cramming before deadlines. Sometimes I'd go days without touching course material, then have marathon sessions. It wasn't healthy, but with everything going on emotionally, I just couldn't maintain regularity. Deadlines became my main driver rather than any real schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even when I wasn't really in the right headspace. The guilt of falling behind would push me to at least check what I was missing. On better days, I genuinely wanted to watch the videos because they helped me visualize concepts.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, check my phone, then open the LMS before breakfast. It happened because I was genuinely interested in a particular module and the videos were really well-made. But that routine broke down when my anxiety spiked and I started avoiding anything that reminded me of how behind I was. The habit didn't stick unfortunately.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline, honestly. I'm a visual learner, so watching someone explain concepts with diagrams and animations made everything click better than reading. I spent over four hours watching videos because that's how I process information best. The discussion boards were okay when people actually engaged meaningfully, but I didn't post much myself. Anything with visual elements felt worth my time.
Q6: I mostly ignored the dense reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was exhausting. Some discussion prompts felt too abstract or like they wouldn't help my grade, so I skipped those. I also delayed practice quizzes because they made my anxiety worse when I got answers wrong. It felt safer to just stick with what I knew worked for me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual explanations, worked examples, anything that showed me the 'why' behind concepts. If something felt like busywork or wouldn't impact my grade, I'd push it aside. Time is precious when you're juggling everything with limited resources, so I had to be selective about what I engaged with deeply.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. When you're already anxious about keeping up with required work, optional stuff just adds to the mental load. I'd look at them and think 'that would be nice' but then prioritize what was graded. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that pushed me to complete them even when I didn't want to. Optional ones got sacrificed when things got overwhelming.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos when I had good internet access and watch them later at home where my connection is unreliable. I also took handwritten notes and made my own visual summaries and diagrams. Sometimes I'd screenshot slides and study from my phone's gallery. A lot of my learning happened with materials spread across my bed, completely disconnected from the LMS.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or redrawing diagrams to understand a concept, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd discuss problems with classmates in person or over WhatsApp, working through difficult material together. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay it mentally while doing other things. The LMS only saw me access something once, but I was processing it for much longer.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my internet situation, I had to be strategic. I'd download everything I could when I was on campus or somewhere with good WiFi, then study from those files for days. One video might show as a single view, but I'd have watched it three or four times offline. My actual engagement with materials was way higher than what the system recorded. It was survival mode with limited resources.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt impossible. When my anxiety was at its worst, even opening the LMS felt overwhelming. I remember a few weeks where personal stuff was weighing on me heavily, and I just couldn't focus. The sadness would hit and suddenly studying felt pointless. My attendance dropped because showing up felt like too much some days. Those were the times I fell furthest behind.
Q13: All of those things affected me, honestly. The workload from 17 online courses was intense, and confusion about some concepts made me avoid those topics entirely. Fatigue from anxiety and stress meant I'd stare at screens without absorbing anything. My extracurricular commitments competed for time, and with low resources, I couldn't always access what I needed when I needed it. Everything compounded and made consistent engagement really difficult.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was sinking in. The anxiety created this fog where I was physically present but mentally checked out. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially activities that triggered my anxiety. Practice tests were the worst because failing them made me spiral. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow rarely came with less anxiety. Some readings felt so dense that I'd avoid them hoping they wouldn't be on the exam. The avoidance was a coping mechanism, even though I knew it made things worse long-term.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act sometimes. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage, even through the anxiety. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates. It wasn't ideal, but that external pressure cut through the fog when nothing else could. Without deadlines, I might have drifted even more than I did.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for days. I started being careful about when I checked grades, trying to do it when I felt emotionally prepared. The feedback mattered, but my emotional state determined how I received it.
Q18: The course structure with its modules and clear progression helped me know what to focus on. Having videos organized by topic made it easier to find what I needed. But the sheer volume of 17 courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention the structure assumed. I'd engage deeply with some modules and barely touch others. The structure was good, but my capacity to follow it was limited by everything else happening.
Q19: Visual materials and clearly organized modules made engagement easier. When I could see a roadmap of what was coming, I felt less anxious. What made it harder was when instructions were unclear or when I had to dig through multiple pages to find what I needed. Discussion requirements felt forced sometimes. The course worked better for me when it accommodated visual learners and didn't assume everyone had constant internet access.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly because they don't understand, or they're anxious and checking things obsessively. Submitting work requires a different kind of energy than just engaging with materials. When you're overwhelmed, you might consume content without being able to synthesize it into assignments.
Q21: That resonates with me too. My exam score was decent even though my attendance was low, because I studied effectively offline. Someone might understand material deeply from a few focused sessions rather than constant LMS activity. The system can't see the quality of engagement, only the quantity. I learned a lot from downloaded materials and peer discussions that never registered anywhere official.
Q22: Logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on. They capture deadline-driven behavior pretty accurately. But they miss the depth of engagement, the emotional state behind the actions, the offline work, the peer learning. They can't tell if someone watched a video while crying from stress or while genuinely excited to learn. The numbers are just shadows of what's actually happening.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when your mind is actively working on problems, making connections, wanting to know more. For me, true engagement happened in quiet moments with my handwritten notes, not necessarily when I was logged in. It's a mental state of curiosity and investment that can't really be measured by clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my emotional journey through this course. The anxiety attacks before exams, the sadness that made some weeks feel impossible, the small victories when something finally clicked. It wouldn't show the peer support I relied on, the offline study sessions, the way I had to work around limited resources. It wouldn't capture that I passed despite everything working against me, and that felt like a real achievement.
